audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel / Shemuel Bet chapter 3, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for prolonged civil war, David's sons and plural marriages, Rizpah accusation, Abner's defection, Michal's forced transfer and Paltiel's grief, tribal negotiations, Joab's extrajudicial killing of Abner, David's disease/disability curse, public mourning, and political disavowal.

verses.restored: War was long between house of Saul and house of David. David grew stronger and stronger, while house of Saul grew weaker and weaker.
Sons were born to David in Hebron. His firstborn was Amnon, by Ahinoam the Jezreelite.
His second was Chileab, by Abigail wife of Nabal the Carmelite; the third was Absalom son of Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur.
The fourth was Adonijah son of Haggith; the fifth was Shephatiah son of Abital.
The sixth was Ithream, by Eglah wife of David. These were born to David in Hebron.
While war continued between house of Saul and house of David, Abner was making himself strong in house of Saul.
Saul had a concubine, and her name was Rizpah daughter of Aiah. Ish-bosheth said to Abner, Why did you go in to my father's concubine?

Abner became very angry over words of Ish-bosheth and said, Am I a dog's head belonging to Judah? This day I do covenant-kindness with house of Saul your father, with his brothers and companions, and have not delivered you into hand of David; yet today you charge me with guilt concerning this woman.
Thus may Divine do to Abner, and thus add, if, as YHWH swore to David, I do not do so for him:
to transfer kingdom from house of Saul and establish throne of David over Israel and over Judah, from Dan even to Beersheba.
Ish-bosheth could not answer Abner another word, because he feared him.
Abner sent messengers to David in his place, saying, Whose is land? Saying also, Make your covenant with me, and look, my hand will be with you to turn all Israel toward you.
David said, Good, I will make covenant with you. One thing I ask from you: you shall not see my face unless you first bring Michal daughter of Saul when you come to see my face.
David sent messengers to Ish-bosheth son of Saul, saying, Give me my wife Michal, whom I betrothed to myself for one hundred foreskins of Philistines.
Ish-bosheth sent and took her from her husband, from Paltiel son of Laish.
Her husband walked with her, going and weeping after her as far as Bahurim. Abner said to him, Go, return. And he returned.
Abner had spoken with elders of Israel, saying, In times past you were seeking David as king over you.
Now act, because YHWH has spoken concerning David, saying, By hand of David my servant I will save my people Israel from hand of Philistines and from hand of all their enemies.
Abner also spoke in ears of Benjamin. Abner also went to speak in ears of David at Hebron all that was good in eyes of Israel and in eyes of whole house of Benjamin.
Abner came to David at Hebron, and with him twenty men. David made a feast for Abner and for men who were with him.

Abner said to David, I will arise and go and gather all Israel to my lord the king, and they will cut covenant with you, and you will reign over all that your soul desires. David sent Abner away, and he went in peace.
Look, servants of David and Joab came from a raid and brought much spoil with them. Abner was not with David in Hebron, because David had sent him away, and he had gone in peace.
Joab and all army that was with him came, and they told Joab, saying, Abner son of Ner came to king, and he sent him away, and he went in peace.
Joab came to king and said, What have you done? Look, Abner came to you. Why is this: you sent him away, and he has gone?
You know Abner son of Ner, that he came to deceive you and to know your going out and your coming in and to know all that you are doing.
Joab went out from David and sent messengers after Abner, and they brought him back from cistern of Sirah. David did not know.
Abner returned to Hebron. Joab turned him aside into middle of gate to speak with him quietly, and struck him there in abdomen, so that he died, for blood of Asahel his brother.
Afterward David heard and said, I and my kingdom are guiltless before YHWH forever from blood of Abner son of Ner.

Let it whirl upon head of Joab and upon all house of his father. Let there never be cut off from house of Joab one with a discharge, one with skin disease, one who holds a spindle/staff, one who falls by sword, or one lacking bread.
So Joab and Abishai his brother killed Abner because he had put Asahel their brother to death in battle at Gibeon.
David said to Joab and to all people who were with him, Tear your garments, gird yourselves with sackcloth, and mourn before Abner. King David walked behind bier.
They buried Abner in Hebron. King lifted his voice and wept at tomb of Abner, and all people wept.
King lamented for Abner and said, Should Abner die as a fool dies?
Your hands were not bound and your feet were not brought into bronze chains. As one falls before children of evil, you fell. And all people wept over him again.
All people came to cause David to eat bread while it was still day, but David swore, saying, Thus may Divine do to me and thus add, if before sun goes down I taste bread or anything.
All people took notice, and it was good in their eyes, as everything king did was good in eyes of all people.
All people and all Israel knew on that day that it was not from king to put Abner son of Ner to death.
King said to his servants, Do you not know that a prince and a great one has fallen this day in Israel?
I am tender/weak this day, though anointed king, and these men, sons of Zeruiah, are too hard for me. May YHWH repay one who does evil according to his evil.

translation_notes: The chapter presents dynastic reproduction through six sons and six named mothers. Women and children must not be reduced to succession inventory.
Rizpah is the subject of a political accusation between men, yet the text gives her no speech or consent. Concubine status does not make sexual access a legitimate token of sovereignty.
Abner changes allegiance after his authority is challenged. His theological language and promise to deliver Israel may combine conviction, anger, survival, and ambition.
David conditions alliance on the return of Michal, invoking a bride-price of mutilated enemy bodies. Neither prior marriage nor royal claim establishes her present consent.

Michal is taken from Paltiel, who follows weeping until Abner orders him away. The scene exposes state power tearing an intimate household apart; it should not be romanticized as rightful restoration.
Joab lures Abner into private conversation and kills him without trial. Blood revenge and security claims do not create due process.

David's curse weaponizes genital discharge, skin disease, mobility dependence, violent death, and hunger as hereditary shame. It must not support ableism, disease stigma, or collective punishment of descendants.

David publicly disavows the killing and genuinely mourns, while also benefiting politically from Abner's death. Public grief does not settle questions of command responsibility or political advantage.
David calls himself weak despite being anointed. Anointing without capacity to restrain one's commanders is not accountable governance.
safety_note: Maximum safeguards: Center Rizpah and Michal as persons rather than sovereignty markers. Do not justify forced marital transfer, blood revenge, private execution, hereditary curses, disease stigma, or leader claims of helplessness. David's mourning matters, but it does not replace investigation and restraint of Joab.
